speaker: interviewer
text: This is February 29, 1996. I'm doing an interview with Nathan Aptekar. The interviewer is Pamela Lane, in Houston, Texas, a And we'll be conducting this interview in English. My name is Pamela Lane, P-A-M-E-L-A L-A-N-E. Today's date is February 29, 1996. I'm conducting an interview with Nathan Aptekar. This interview is being conducted in English. All right. What is your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nathan Aptekar, A-P-T-E-K-A-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your date of birth?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zdzilowice, a small town in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell it for us, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Z-D-Z-I-L-O-W-I-C-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell us a little bit about your family? What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eljezer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: E-L-J-E-Z-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your father do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: Salesman.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he sell?

speaker: interviewee
text: Coals and, what do they call it? Paint, but the paint came in a type of stones. You had to put them in the water to create paint. That was not so easy, like today, you go to the store and buy already paint.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you live when you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the city, Lodz, L-O-D-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you move to Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was four years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how large a city that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the biggest industrial city in Poland when it comes to clothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember anything about the Jewish community when you were a little boy there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we used to go with my father to the synagogues.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a large Jewish community there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Large, yes. Very large.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was your home located?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 87 Street [POLISH]. In Polish they call [POLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that in a Jewish neighborhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about your neighborhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a typical cluster of homes, five, six, story building, with a big yard in the middle. All the windows were facing to the yard. Many times peddlers used to come in, musicians, to throw down from the windows some charity for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the children do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Playing in the yard, going to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of games did you play?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly Poland was soccer, was one of the famous games.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did all the Jewish children play together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly, yes. They could not play with anybody else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience antisemitism there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Almost every day on the way to school. If I was lucky, I was not beaten up. My books were ripped out many times, the homework disappeared. Until I got older, and I ask all the kids to go with me together, not to go alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think about being treated like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, if one thinks today, it's difficult to understand, when you go to school you have to be afraid to be beaten up from your neighbor kids. And was calling names, like in English it would come out, dirty Jew, in Polish, [POLISH]. And this was a daily, what they call your name, [NON-ENGLISH]. That means, Jews go to Israel, Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Awful, depressed. When you came to school, you actually could not study after going through this type of experience.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have trouble in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had to do two years to finish the first grade.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about your mother. What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chaja, C-H-A-J-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a woman was she?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was working in a clothing factory. And the rest, doing the cooking, taking care of the kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a religious family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about Shabbat at your home.

speaker: interviewee
text: After coming back from Daven with my father, always the candles were lit. We all make kiddish, and normal food and [NON-ENGLISH] to sing at the meal, and after the meal. It was strictly, strictly religious, a kosher home.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your brothers and sisters? Did you have brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: One brother, and two sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother's name is Icek, older sister's name is Regina, and the middle one, Ester.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now tell me in ages, who was the oldest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Regina, she is now 75. Ester, is probably about five years younger. And my brother, Icek, is four years older from me.

speaker: interviewer
text: You are the baby?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did your mother like to do? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Knitting, whenever she had time, she liked to sit and knit, making sweaters, scarves, gloves. Shawls in Poland is called [POLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your sisters and brothers, did you get along well with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I could say, yes. Whatever time we had, we were all knitting together. And the rest, they were going to school. Same time like I was going, but they went to different schools.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get a Jewish education also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, before even I went to the public school, I was having a [NON-ENGLISH] they call it in Jewish, a teacher that was teaching me Hebrew. Davening which I knew very well at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when things started to change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nine years.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were nine years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1939 When the first sirens started and the bombs were flying. And I remember like today when I looked out from the hiding places we were on, we were seeing the-- some Polish people, whatever, worked together with the Germans. I don't remember the name on them, but they were staying on the roofs, signaling to the planes where to bomb. [NON-ENGLISH]. And Lodz was very famous for it. So the German has a very easy job knowing exactly where to hit. And after two weeks they just came into the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the bombing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were hiding can in the basements of the big buildings. But it wasn't big bombings over there, just they were bombing the electric station, which was right in the corner from us, the post office, government buildings mostly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you afraid?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. Could a nine-year-old guy not be afraid?

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened after the bombings?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the bombings started, the Germans came into the city. And the first order of the day was that all the Jews have to wear yellow belts on the arm.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think about having to wear this yellow band?

speaker: interviewee
text: Honestly, at that time, as a young kid, I figured it's something like a treat. I put it on and walked out in the street, the first one. Till my mother pulled me back not to go. I figured it's something cute to wear, the band. I didn't understand. But then, passed by, maybe a week or 10 days, was when all of the Jews to concentrate in one place to the ghetto, that they made. I think Lodz was the first Jewish ghetto created.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family move to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We, in the nighttime, escaped from the city through walking out. It took us several days to reach the town, Zdzilowice, where my mother was born over there. And the grandparents were still living.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that where you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me about that. When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in the end of 1939. I remember it was very cold. Difficult to remember dates. But I think it was around October.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you walked all the way?

speaker: interviewee
text: We walked several days. And then we with farmers, when they was going to the bazaars like here. How do you call bazaar? Flea markets. And we had the rides with them slowly, till we arrived to the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened when you got to the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: We moved in to the grandparents. And we were living over there. That was very difficult from dislocating from the place, from a big city, without any job, without mean of getting jobs. But somehow the parents managed. Me and my brother was going to the woods, summertime, to collect leftover branches from trees, to having for cooking and heating for the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your grandparents' names, that you lived with?

speaker: interviewee
text: The grandmother was name was Ita. And the grandparent name was Moshe.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was your mother's or father's?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother's.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Aptekar.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Klinkofman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that for us, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: K-L-I-N-K-O-F-M-A-N

speaker: interviewer
text: And at their home, were you able to continue your education?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. None of the Jewish were allowed to go to any school after the Germans stopped there. So by that time, all the education was finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just going to the woods to collect the wood, which I mentioned before, almost every day summertime, to have it for the winter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay with your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until October, November 1942, when all the war, said all the Jews have to concentrate in big cities to be relocated. That was the normal, telling us that we get relocated to places where there's jobs and other thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember if your family ever talked about what was happening to the Jews there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I do remember there was discussions when I went with my father to the synagogue. Nobody actually knew exactly what happened. Nobody like what's happening. Everybody was looking for places to wherever it's still not Judenrein, but that people can still live. And my parents picked a city not far from Lublin. The city's name is Turobin, T-O-R-O-B-I-N. We went over there. We had relatives. And we were not the only one in the city. It was so crowded that every family had maybe 10 families living with them, from relatives, from all over towns, they were coming over there. We were still allowed to live normal.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you move there?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was by the end of 1942 when they made the Judenrein. I think it's the end. It's difficult to remember exactly the dates, but each area was different dates. But in this city, Turobin, we could not stay too long. From there was the order that all has to go to Majdanek, to concentrate. And I remembered the last day we went to the shul, synagogue, to pray, to say Tehillim. Tehillim is a special prayer in the Bible. And for every misfortune, funerals, you say Tehillim special prayers. And we were praying that maybe God will help us and the German would change their mind not to go the Majdanek. We knew already it's something not correct. I remember like today, that the rabbi had a speech by the end of the Tehillim praying. And he said in Hebrew, [HEBREW]. This is, in English, [HEBREW] would be friends, or it came the time to go to be sacrificed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think of that when he said that? What did you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: I could not agree with him at all, being 9 years old, or 10 years. This was something. At that time, I was10 years old. I could not understand how to go to be sacrificed. Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: So on the way home, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember like today, my father was crying. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [SOBBING] And when we come back, he said to mother, take the kids and go away. You will survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the morning, we got dressed like farmers. We took shovels. There are special shovels where they dig potatoes. It's like the end of the stick, the shovel is like this for digging potatoes. We took on the arms. And we went out of the city. It was the only bridge the German wasn't watching. But they did not recognize. My father did not want to take off the beard. He was very religious. We, the boys, me and my brother, cut off the [HEBREW]. And we put up the vest is like the farmers. And we left. And we came back to the town, Zdzilowice. We went into a Polish family, a gentile family, by the name, Matisek, M-A-T-I-S-E-K. They kept us in the attic from the house, that's [INAUDIBLE] there. After we were there upstairs in this attic about three weeks. In this three weeks, they dug out a bunker behind the kitchen, going to the bedroom. That was a two room house. In the corner of the kitchen was a fireplace like here. And they kept the wood, where the wood is, they made the entrance to the bunker. And this was a dug bunker. We were there for six months.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: He went with the rabbi, with all his friends, to Majdanek. And that was the end of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in hiding, what was that like for your family, when you lived in the attic and in the bunker? What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the bunker we were sitting all the time inside. But this was tremendous, how to say it? It was very difficult to leave and not to get killed from Polish bandits, whatever, just chasing to get Jews. They get 5 kilo sugar for it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the family, whatever food they had, this was a poor family, Matisek. We were with them. They only give us a couple of boiled potatoes a day. And we survived on this one.

speaker: interviewee
text: After the six months, we started going out in the night to get some food. And mostly, we went out, my older sister, Regina, and me, as the youngest one. We went out early in the morning, 4 o'clock, and came back very late in the night, nobody to follow us where we're going.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you find food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We went to some gentile families. They were very kind enough. Even they would endanger themselves, they were giving us food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you mentioned earlier there was a bounty for Jews? Tell me a little more about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the first the firemen in the city, in the town, they called them in Polish, the [POLISH]. But this was the volunteer fire department. They were catching. For each Jew what they caught, they got 5 kilo sugar.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who gave them the sugar?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans. And there were other ones, didn't care for the sugar. They were just catching and killing. As a matter of fact, this family, Matisek, where we were hiding in the bunker, their grandson was one of the biggest killers in town. Every couple night, he came in the evening sitting, and talking, and explaining how many he killed, and how he killed, with a hatchet, with a shovel. And we were hearing everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how your family knew which gentiles wouldn't hurt you, which ones would help you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But we know everybody. We found out from the family where we were. But then, who is the killers and who is not? And we know the people who used to live over there. So we know who to go. And by the end of the six months being the bunker, the fact that we had to leave this place-- let me retract that. One night, my sister, Regina, left in the morning for food. And that time was really quiet in town, so Mrs. Matisek ask us that we can already sit a little bit in the kitchen the night to straighten out. There was no light, nothing. And about 10, 11 o'clock in the night, we hear a big noise. Four of, I think it was four of them, from the bandits, they caught my sister, Regina. And they were beating her. They followed her, actually, they burst into the house together with her. And they find mother, sister, my brother, sitting in the kitchen. I, at that time, jumped under the bed, slide over. They did not see me. They were beating them with the other end of the rifle, that the blood was all over the wall. Actually, they left them. They were laying without movement. And she was begging them, Mrs. Matisek, to them in Polish. She sweared them by God, leave them alone. They already have death, like this. And finally they left.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when they left, I crawled out from under the bed. And we took some water. We washed them, Mrs. Matisek and me, until they come to themself.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went on the attic, not in the house already, just on the barn, where they had to keep the cows. We were there maybe a week. But we were afraid to already to be over there, should they come back and finish off. But they were in no position to move. In that time we changed. We went to another family by the name of Golec.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we went into them, it was by the end of 1942. It was around April, May, of 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at the second family, where did you did live?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the top of where the cows are in the barn, upstairs, until it took three, four, weeks to build a bunker under where the cows stayed. And many times, when the cows were peeing, it was straight going down to us. So we had not only sit dry, we were sitting in wet. And we were there almost five, close to six months.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you eat while you were there? How did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly two, three, potatoes daily, and some bread and water. We had a small opening to the outside, maybe 2 inches, for fresh air. And this was the light and the fresh air.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We were afraid, after the beating already, to leave until after that time. But by the end of 1943, I started already to go out in town to people what I knew. And several of them was putting me food under a stone. A family, Pison, they used to be our neighbors. And he was afraid. He didn't want his kids to know that he is helping me. So he put food under a stone. And he said, Nathan, if the food will be there, I know that you're not alive. If the food is not there, I'll put other food. And this was going on for months like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pison, P-I-S-O-N. I visited the family last year, but only the kids. And the older daughter, when she used to play with me, when we were nine, 10 old, she said, I knew my father was helping you. Needless to say, we were very well. And when we came to them, they didn't know what to do for us. This was now a year ago, after the war. But going back to that time when I was going out in the town already, we were hearing one evening shots. And we found out the next day that my mother's sister, and her husband, and two of their boys were killed by the people who hide them, not far from us, maybe six houses away. They took them out on the field. Everybody was hearing the way-- my uncle's name was Chaim. I remember like today. He was begging them, calling by the name of the people. I will get you whatever I can get together. I have hidden gold here, and there. Just don't kill me. Nothing helped. They shot and buried them in the place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the names of the people who shot them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. No, I don't remember the name now, but I can find out the name. But the question was that time, that we know that they will not to let any witnesses, any relatives to survive after the war. So I decided to go to them for food. And they were very, very polite, invite me to eat breakfast with them together. And they were asking where my family is. I said, we all separate. One doesn't know from the other, in case he gets caught. But if one will get caught, the families will burn them down. And I continue coming to them for months for food. Especially for them to think that we don't know that they did it. So they did not look for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how old were you at this time at the end of 1943?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was at that time already 13 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think about that at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The only thing what a person in that position was thinking, is how to survive, not to be caught. And the biggest wish was to go to sleep and not to wake up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: About every day, you're finding out who else was killed from the people that they were hiding. And the numbers was coming down drastically. And we know all these people. And the question was, who was next? And there were everyday, was a next person. And they wasn't killed like a rifle. They were mostly killed like they're doing in Mexico with hatchets, or the other. In our area that's what it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Trying to survive. Not to let yourself to be caught. Going out in the morning, one day I collected some bread from several people. It was too early to go back to our place. So I went under a barn, where there were some blocks, staying under the floor, and fell asleep. When I woke up, I didn't have the bread. I'm looking outside to see a duck is holding the bag, the sack. And he's still eating. So I jumped on him. And it's left over, one piece. So the duck walked away. He wasn't hungry. So he didn't bite me. And I came back with one piece of bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you said you stayed under the barn.

speaker: interviewee
text: Until late in the night. Then I returned to the place.

speaker: interviewer
text: But your family lived under the barn until the end of 1943?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the end of 1943, these people didn't want to hold us any longer. They were afraid. And we went to another Polish family, but they did not have any food to give us. So the only food what we can organize is to go around like I did. One day, me and my brother went. And we figured, we'll bring food, whatever we can. And we took ourselves green peas, what they were keeping under a house, to dry. So we took sheets. We put the green peas inside. Yeah, green peas it was. And we carried on the shoulder like inexperienced thieves. And probably we were leaving signs. Until the next day in the morning, we hear something, a noise outside. We were in the attic. We're looking down. Guys with such a long sticks, and said, we'll have to kill these thieves. They didn't know who did it. And they came actually into the barn. And I was that time, me, my brother-- we both brought the green peas-- and mother. The split decision was to rip off the roof. It was a straw roof. Me and my brother jumped down. And we run away. The caught my mother. They didn't do nothing to mother, but they knew that we escaped.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to pause for just a moment and change tapes.
